name: Greenwich Mean Time
dstRules: UK/Ireland/Portugal observe DST as BST/IST/WEST (UTC+1 in summer); Iceland and West African countries do not
description: The reference timezone for the world, centered on the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, London. All other time zones are defined as offsets from UTC. The UK switches to British Summer Time (BST, UTC+1) from late March to late October.

name: Central European Time
dstRules: European countries observe DST as CEST (UTC+2 in summer, Mar–Oct); African countries do not
description: The most populous time zone in Europe, covering the EU's economic heartland. Major cities include Paris, Berlin, Rome, Madrid, Amsterdam, and Lagos. The EU has debated abolishing seasonal clock changes since 2019.

name: Australian Eastern Standard Time
dstRules: NSW/Victoria/Tasmania/ACT observe DST (UTC+11 in summer, Oct–Apr); Queensland, Guam, and PNG do not
description: Covers Australia's most populous eastern states and major Pacific territories. Sydney and Melbourne are in this zone, though Queensland notably refuses DST, creating seasonal time differences between Brisbane and Sydney.

name: Lord Howe Standard Time
dstRules: Observes 30-minute DST shift (UTC+11 in summer, Oct–Apr) — the only place using a 30-minute DST change
description: Used exclusively by Lord Howe Island, a tiny Australian territory in the Tasman Sea with about 400 residents. Uniquely, Lord Howe Island advances clocks by only 30 minutes for DST (to UTC+11), rather than the standard one-hour shift.

name: Solomon Islands Time
dstRules: Generally no DST observed; Norfolk Island abolished DST in 2015
description: Covers the western Pacific islands and Russia's far eastern regions. The Solomon Islands, site of crucial WWII battles, and New Caledonia, known for its vast lagoon, are the main Pacific territories in this zone.

name: New Zealand Standard Time
dstRules: New Zealand observes DST (UTC+13 in summer, Sep–Apr); Fiji also observes DST
description: Covers New Zealand and numerous Pacific island nations. Auckland is the largest city in this zone. New Zealand was one of the first countries to adopt standard time in 1868, and it's among the first to see each new day (excluding UTC+13/+14 zones).

name: Chatham Island Standard Time
dstRules: Observes DST (UTC+13:45 in summer, Sep–Apr)
description: Used exclusively by New Zealand's Chatham Islands, a remote archipelago about 800 km east of the South Island. With a population of about 600, it maintains its 45-minute offset from New Zealand time, established because of its eastern longitude.

name: Tonga Time
dstRules: Samoa observes DST (UTC+14 in summer); Tonga does not
description: One of the first time zones to see each new day. Samoa switched from UTC-11 to UTC+13 in December 2011, skipping an entire day to align with its major trading partners Australia and New Zealand rather than the Americas.

name: Line Islands Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: The furthest ahead timezone in the world. The Line Islands, part of the Republic of Kiribati, are the first inhabited places to welcome each new day. Kiribati moved these islands from UTC-10 to UTC+14 on December 31, 1994, to unify the nation on the same calendar day.

name: Eastern European Time
dstRules: European countries observe DST as EEST (UTC+3 in summer); Israel uses IDT; African countries generally do not
description: Spans from Scandinavia through Eastern Europe to southern Africa. This zone bridges three continents, covering major cities like Athens, Cairo, Helsinki, Johannesburg, and Bucharest. South Africa, the largest economy in Africa, operates on this time year-round.

name: Moscow Standard Time
dstRules: Russia and Turkey permanently on UTC+3 (no DST); most countries in this zone do not observe DST
description: A major timezone spanning from Eastern Europe through the Middle East to East Africa. Moscow moved to permanent UTC+3 in 2014, and Turkey followed suit in 2016. Riyadh, Nairobi, and Istanbul are among the largest cities in this zone.

name: Iran Standard Time
dstRules: Iran observes DST (UTC+04:30 in summer, ~Mar–Sep)
description: Used exclusively by Iran, one of the most populous countries with a half-hour offset timezone. Iran's calendar follows the Solar Hijri system, and its DST schedule differs from Western conventions, typically starting around the March equinox.

name: Gulf Standard Time
dstRules: Generally no DST observed in this zone
description: Covers the Persian Gulf states, South Caucasus, and western Indian Ocean islands. Dubai and Abu Dhabi have become global hubs for commerce and aviation, strategically positioned between European and Asian time zones.

name: Afghanistan Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: Used exclusively by Afghanistan. This half-hour offset was adopted in 1945. Afghanistan's position at the crossroads of Central and South Asia is reflected in its unique timezone, bridging the Gulf and South Asian time zones.

name: Pakistan Standard Time
dstRules: Generally no DST observed; Pakistan briefly used DST in 2002 and 2008–2009
description: Spans from the Ural Mountains in Russia through Central Asia to Pakistan. Karachi and Lahore are among the most populous cities in this zone. The historical Silk Road traversed much of this timezone's territory.

name: India Standard Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: One of the world's most populous time zones, covering over 1.4 billion people in India alone. India uses a single time zone despite spanning roughly 30° of longitude. Sri Lanka adopted UTC+05:30 in 2006 to align with India.

name: Nepal Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: The only timezone in the world with a 45-minute offset from UTC. Nepal adopted this unique offset in 1986 to distinguish itself from India's UTC+05:30. The offset is based on the meridian of Kathmandu (85°19' E), calculated as 5 hours and 45 minutes ahead of GMT.

name: Bangladesh Standard Time
dstRules: Generally no DST observed; Bangladesh briefly used DST in 2009
description: Covers the densely populated Bangladesh delta region and the vast steppes of Central Asia. Dhaka is one of the most densely populated cities in the world. Kazakhstan's largest city, Almaty, also operates on this time.

name: Myanmar Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: Used primarily by Myanmar (Burma), a country of over 54 million people in Southeast Asia. This half-hour offset timezone reflects Myanmar's geographic position between the Indian subcontinent and mainland Southeast Asia.

name: Indochina Time
dstRules: No DST observed in any country using this timezone
description: Covers the heart of mainland Southeast Asia and western Indonesia. Bangkok, Jakarta, and Ho Chi Minh City are major economic centers. The combined population of this timezone exceeds 400 million people.

name: China Standard Time
dstRules: Generally no DST observed; China uses a single timezone for the entire country despite spanning 5 geographic zones
description: The world's most populous time zone, covering over 1.7 billion people. China controversially uses a single timezone (Beijing Time) across its vast territory. Singapore, despite being geographically at UTC+7, uses UTC+8 for economic alignment with China and Malaysia.

name: Australian Central Western Time
dstRules: Unofficially observes DST (+09:45 in summer); not recognized by Australian federal law
description: One of the world's rarest time zones, used by a tiny community along the Western Australia–South Australia border near Eucla. With only a few hundred residents, this 45-minute offset zone is an unofficial compromise between Perth (UTC+8) and Adelaide (UTC+9:30).

name: Japan Standard Time
dstRules: No DST observed in Japan or South Korea; Japan abolished DST after WWII occupation ended
description: Covers the economic powerhouses of Japan and South Korea. Tokyo is the world's most populous metropolitan area. Japan experimented with DST during the US occupation (1948–1951) but abandoned it due to public opposition.

name: Australian Central Standard Time
dstRules: South Australia observes DST (UTC+10:30 in summer, Oct–Apr); Northern Territory does not
description: Covers Australia's central states. Adelaide (South Australia) observes DST while Darwin (Northern Territory) does not, creating a seasonal time difference within the same standard timezone. The half-hour offset dates back to colonial-era telegraph coordination.

name: Azores Standard Time
dstRules: Azores observe DST (UTC±0 in summer); Cape Verde does not
description: Covers the Azores archipelago in the mid-Atlantic and the Cape Verde islands off West Africa. The Azores serve as a crucial waypoint for transatlantic travel and are known for their volcanic landscapes and mild maritime climate.

name: Hawaii-Aleutian Standard Time
dstRules: Hawaii does not observe DST; Aleutian Islands observe DST (UTC-9 in summer)
description: Covers the Hawaiian Islands and parts of the Aleutian Islands chain. Hawaii permanently uses standard time, making it one of only two US states (along with Arizona) that do not observe DST.

name: Samoa Standard Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: Covers American Samoa and Niue in the South Pacific. American Samoa is one of the few US territories that does not observe daylight saving time.

name: Baker Island Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: The furthest west timezone, observed only on uninhabited US minor outlying islands in the central Pacific Ocean. Baker and Howland Islands are among the last places on Earth to see each new day.

name: South Georgia Time
countries: South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands (UK)
dstRules: No DST observed
description: One of the least populated time zones, covering primarily the remote South Atlantic islands. Fernando de Noronha, a Brazilian archipelago known for its pristine beaches, and the sub-Antarctic South Georgia island are the main inhabited territories.

name: Brasília Time
dstRules: Generally no DST; Greenland observes DST in some areas
description: Covers eastern South America including Brazil's most populous regions. São Paulo and Buenos Aires, two of the largest cities in the Southern Hemisphere, operate on this time. Brazil abandoned DST in 2019.

name: Newfoundland Standard Time
dstRules: Observes DST (UTC-2:30 in summer, Mar–Nov)
description: One of the few half-hour offset zones, used by Newfoundland and Labrador in eastern Canada. St. John's, the provincial capital, is the oldest and most easterly city in North America. The half-hour offset dates back to 1884.

name: Atlantic Standard Time
dstRules: Canada/Bermuda observe DST (UTC-3 in summer); most South American countries do not
description: Spans the Caribbean, Atlantic Canada, and western South America. This zone includes a diverse mix of cultures from the maritime provinces of Canada to the tropical islands of the Caribbean and the Andes mountains.

name: Eastern Standard Time
dstRules: US/Canada observe DST (UTC-4 in summer, Mar–Nov); South American countries generally do not
description: The most economically significant timezone in the Americas, home to New York City, Washington DC, Toronto, and the major financial markets. Covers the entire eastern seaboard of the US and extends into South America.

name: Central Standard Time
dstRules: US/Canada/Mexico observe DST (UTC-5 in summer); Central American countries do not
description: The most populous North American time zone, covering the heartland of the United States, most of Mexico, and Central America. Major cities include Chicago, Houston, Dallas, Mexico City, and San Antonio.

name: Mountain Standard Time
dstRules: Most areas observe DST (UTC-6 in summer); Arizona does not observe DST
description: Covers the Rocky Mountain region of North America. Arizona famously does not observe daylight saving time (except the Navajo Nation), creating a unique patchwork of time-keeping in the US Southwest.

name: Pacific Standard Time
dstRules: Observes DST (UTC-7 in summer, Mar–Nov in US/Canada)
description: Covers the Pacific coast of North America including major cities like Los Angeles, San Francisco, Seattle, and Vancouver. Home to Silicon Valley and Hollywood, this timezone spans a region of enormous economic and cultural influence.

name: Alaska Standard Time
dstRules: Alaska observes DST (UTC-8 in summer, Mar–Nov)
description: Covers most of Alaska, the largest US state. Alaska's extreme northern latitude means summer days can last over 20 hours, while winter days may have only a few hours of twilight.

name: Marquesas Time
dstRules: No DST observed
description: One of the few half-hour offset time zones, used exclusively by the Marquesas Islands in French Polynesia. This remote archipelago in the South Pacific is known for its dramatic volcanic landscapes.
